facts: Sea turtles can hold their breath for 4 to 7 hours while sleeping or resting by slowing their heart rate to as little as one beat every nine minutes.
Slider turtles get their name from their habit of sliding off logs or rocks into the water when startled.
Tortoises are land-adapted and generally cannot swim well; they can drown in deep water.
Turtles rely heavily on vibration and sight rather than hearing, which is why they're often easy to startle but hard to sneak up on quietly.
Some turtle species live over 50 years in the wild, and certain tortoises can live well over a century.
Freshwater turtles that seem slow can burst into fast, sudden movement when startled or fleeing a predator.
Turtles as a lineage date back roughly 230 to 260 million years, predating most dinosaurs, and survived the mass extinction that wiped out non-avian dinosaurs 66 million years ago.
A turtle's shell is fused to its ribs and spine as part of its skeleton, so it can never be removed or outgrown.
Most freshwater turtles are omnivores, eating a mix of plants, insects, and small aquatic animals.
UVB light is essential for captive turtles to synthesize vitamin D3 and properly process calcium.
Iceberg lettuce has very little nutritional value for turtles and is a poor staple food.
Red-eared sliders and other freshwater turtles are often visually drawn to red and orange colored foods.
Basking in sunlight helps turtles regulate body temperature, aids digestion, and supports shell and bone health.
Sea turtles use Earth's magnetic field as an internal compass to navigate migrations spanning thousands of miles.
Plastic pollution is a major threat to sea turtles, who often mistake floating plastic bags for jellyfish.
Turtles have no teeth; they use a hard, beak-like mouth to bite and tear food.
Hatchling sea turtles instinctively crawl toward the brighter, more open horizon after emerging from the nest, which is why artificial beachfront lighting can disorient them.
Turtles can slow their heart rate dramatically underwater, sometimes to one beat every several minutes, to conserve oxygen.
Tortoises are built for walking on land, with thick, columnar legs rather than the flippers or webbed feet of aquatic turtles.
Turtles are capable of associative learning and can recognize feeding routines, sounds, or the people who regularly feed them.
In most turtles the sex of hatchlings is set by nest temperature rather than genetics; cooler nests tend to produce more males and warmer nests produce more females.
For red-eared sliders, eggs incubated below about 28°C hatch as all male and eggs above about 31°C hatch as all female, with a narrow middle range producing a mix.
Climate change is skewing some sea turtle populations heavily female, since warming sand raises average nest temperatures.
Female sea turtles typically return to nest on the same beach where they themselves hatched, a behavior called natal homing.
Leatherback sea turtles are the largest living turtle species, can weigh close to 2,000 pounds, and have a flexible, leathery shell instead of a hard carapace.
Leatherbacks are also the deepest-diving sea turtle, with dives recorded beyond 1,000 meters (over 3,000 feet).
Painted turtles and some other freshwater species can survive being frozen for extended periods during winter brumation by drastically slowing their metabolism.
Box turtles have a hinged plastron that lets them close their shell almost completely, sealing out most predators.
Softshell turtles lack the hard, bony scutes of typical turtles and instead have a flattened shell covered in leathery skin.
Common snapping turtles are far more aggressive on land than in water, where they're usually docile and prefer to swim away.
Most turtles have a three-chambered heart, unlike the four-chambered hearts of mammals and birds.
A turtle's growth rings, or scutes, can give a rough estimate of age but aren't fully reliable since wear and growth rate vary.
The Galápagos tortoise is one of the longest-lived vertebrates, with some individuals documented living well over 150 years.
Alligator snapping turtles have a worm-shaped appendage on their tongue that they wiggle to lure fish directly into their jaws.
An alligator snapping turtle's bite is powerful enough to snap a wooden broom handle in half.
Alligator snapping turtles often lie motionless on muddy river bottoms with algae growing on their shells, camouflaging them from prey.
The alligator snapping turtle is the largest freshwater turtle in North America, with males reaching up to 200 pounds.
Hawksbill turtles get their name from their curved, bird-like beak, which lets them reach into narrow reef crevices.
Hawksbill turtles feed mainly on sea sponges, sometimes eating over 1,000 pounds of sponge a year, which helps keep reef sponge growth in balance.
Hawksbill turtle shells were historically prized for 'tortoiseshell' jewelry and ornaments, a major reason the species is now critically endangered.
Green sea turtles are named for the greenish color of their body fat, caused by their seagrass-and-algae diet, not the color of their shell.
Green sea turtles are the only sea turtle species that is strictly herbivorous as an adult.
Green sea turtle hatchlings are carnivorous but shift to an almost entirely plant-based diet as they mature.
Loggerhead sea turtles get their name from their large, block-shaped heads and powerful jaws, used to crush hard-shelled prey like conch and crab.
Kemp's ridley sea turtles are the smallest and most endangered sea turtle species.
Kemp's ridley and olive ridley turtles are known for 'arribadas,' mass synchronized nesting events where thousands of females nest on the same beach within days.
Olive ridley sea turtles are believed to be the most abundant sea turtle species worldwide.
Flatback sea turtles are found only in the waters around Australia and have a distinctively flattened shell with upturned edges.
Diamondback terrapins are the only turtle species in the U.S. that lives exclusively in brackish coastal marshes and estuaries.
Diamondback terrapins get their name from the diamond-shaped, concentric ring pattern on their shell scutes.
Common musk turtles are nicknamed 'stinkpots' because they release a foul-smelling musk from glands when threatened.
Map turtles get their name from the fine, contour-line-like markings on their shells that resemble topographic maps.
Wood turtles are considered one of the more intelligent turtle species and have been observed stomping the ground to lure earthworms to the surface.
The Aldabra giant tortoise is one of the largest tortoise species and can weigh over 500 pounds.
Jonathan, a Seychelles giant tortoise living on the island of St. Helena, is recognized as the oldest known living land animal, estimated to be around 190 years old.
Turtle shells are covered in scutes, keratin plates made of the same material as human fingernails, which grow in layers over the underlying bone.
Softshell turtles can absorb oxygen through specialized skin and the lining of their throat and cloaca, letting them stay submerged for long stretches.
Turtles lack vocal cords in the traditional sense but many species can hiss, grunt, or make other sounds by expelling air.
The pig-nosed turtle has a fleshy, snorkel-like nose adapted for breathing at the water's surface while staying mostly submerged.
Turtles have good color vision and see reds and yellows particularly well, one reason brightly colored foods attract them.
A turtle's plastron pattern and shape can often help identify its sex, species, or even the individual animal.
Male turtles of many species have longer tails and claws than females, adaptations related to mating.
In many turtle species, females grow larger than males, since bigger bodies allow them to carry more eggs.
Turtles bury their eggs in a dug-out nest and then leave; the vast majority of species provide no parental care after that point.
Snapping turtles can live over 40 years and tend to stay defensive even in captivity, since their reduced ability to hide in their shell leaves them relying on aggression instead.
Turtles have a well-developed sense of smell and use tongue and throat movements to draw in water and detect chemical cues from food.
Turtles brumate rather than truly hibernate, entering a state of dramatically reduced activity during cold months rather than continuous deep sleep.
Painted turtles overwintering in mud at the bottom of ponds can survive months with essentially no oxygen by relying on anaerobic metabolism.
Turtle eggs have leathery, flexible shells rather than hard ones, letting the nest pack tightly without eggs cracking.
Some turtle species can store sperm from a single mating for months or even years and still produce fertile eggs later.
Turtles are ectothermic ('cold-blooded'), meaning their body temperature depends on their environment rather than being internally regulated.
Freshwater turtles often bask on logs in groups, sometimes stacking on top of one another to claim a spot in direct sun.
A turtle retracts its head straight back into its shell if it's a cryptodire, or folds it sideways if it's a pleurodire, depending on its family.
Side-necked turtles (pleurodires) are found mainly in the Southern Hemisphere, including South America, Africa, and Australia.
Minor scute damage on a turtle's shell can regenerate over time, though deep shell fractures usually need veterinary care to heal properly.
Box turtles have been documented living within the same small home range, sometimes just a few acres, for their entire lives.
Some box turtles live 50 to 100 years, making them among the longer-lived reptiles kept as pets.
Turtle shell shape varies widely; more domed shells generally belong to land tortoises, while flatter, streamlined shells belong to aquatic swimmers.
Sea turtles cannot retract their heads or flippers into their shells, unlike most freshwater turtles and tortoises.
A sea turtle's front flippers provide the main swimming power, while the back flippers act more like rudders for steering.
Loggerhead turtles have one of the widest ranges of any sea turtle species, found in tropical and temperate waters around the globe.
Baby sea turtles go through a 'swim frenzy' right after hatching, swimming almost continuously for the first 24 to 48 hours to reach deeper, safer water.
Only an estimated 1 in 1,000 sea turtle hatchlings survives to adulthood.
Sea turtles appear to 'cry,' but it isn't emotional — the tears are how they excrete excess salt through specialized glands near their eyes.
Sea turtles' salt glands let them drink seawater directly, unlike most marine reptiles, which get most of their moisture from food.
Many sea turtles nest at night, which helps females avoid predators and the heat of the day.
Some turtles, including certain tortoises, can go for weeks or months without eating by relying on stored fat reserves.
A turtle's shell contains nerve endings, so while it can't feel light touch the way skin does, it can sense pressure and pain through the shell.
Wild turtle populations face major threats from habitat loss, road mortality, the illegal pet trade, and being caught as fishing bycatch.
Turtle excluder devices (TEDs) are trap doors built into shrimp trawl nets that let turtles escape while keeping shrimp inside, reducing bycatch deaths.
Nest predators like raccoons, foxes, and ghost crabs can destroy a large share of turtle eggs before they even hatch.
Many coastal areas now require shielded, turtle-friendly beach lighting to avoid disorienting hatchlings on their way to the water.
The Nile softshell turtle and the giant Asian softshell turtle are among the largest freshwater turtle species in the world.
Sea turtles are found on every continent's coastal waters except Antarctica, ranging through tropical and temperate oceans worldwide.
Turtles have kept a broadly similar body plan for tens of millions of years, earning them the nickname 'living fossils.'
Fossil evidence shows early turtle relatives, like Odontochelys, had a fully formed lower shell (plastron) before evolving a complete upper shell (carapace).
There are roughly 360 living turtle and tortoise species recognized today, spanning oceans, rivers, wetlands, and deserts.
Desert tortoises survive extremely arid climates by digging burrows to escape scorching daytime heat.
Desert tortoises can go up to a year without drinking free water, getting most of their hydration from the plants they eat.
Gopher tortoises dig extensive burrows that hundreds of other species also use, earning them the title of a 'keystone species.'
A gopher tortoise burrow can run over 40 feet long and 10 feet deep.
Red-eared sliders have become an invasive species in parts of the world after being released from the pet trade.
Red-eared sliders are named for the small red stripe behind each eye and their habit of quickly sliding into water when disturbed.
Painted turtles are the most widespread native turtle species in North America.
Painted turtles get their name from the bright red, yellow, and orange markings along the edges of their shell and skin.
Wood turtles roam far from water and are considered more terrestrial than most other freshwater turtles.
A turtle's shell color and pattern can act as camouflage, helping it blend into muddy riverbeds, leaf litter, or sandy ocean floors depending on habitat.
Snapping turtles have a long, flexible neck that can reach nearly halfway across their own shell, giving them surprising striking range.
Musk turtles are among the smallest freshwater turtles in North America, rarely growing beyond about 5 inches.
A female turtle's egg-laying behavior is triggered by hormonal cues, and she may travel significant distances over land to find suitable nesting soil.
Baby turtles use a temporary, sharp structure called a caruncle, or 'egg tooth,' to break through their eggshell; it falls off shortly after hatching.
Sea turtles migrate between distinct feeding grounds and nesting beaches, sometimes covering more than 1,000 miles one-way.
Loggerhead hatchlings from Florida can drift across the entire Atlantic on ocean currents before eventually returning years later as juveniles.
Snapping turtles play an important ecological role as scavengers, helping keep freshwater ecosystems clean by eating dead animal matter.
A turtle's shell growth slows significantly with age, which is part of why very old individuals can sometimes be identified by their shell wear.

Terrapins, turtles, and tortoises all belong to the same order, Testudines; the different names mainly reflect habitat, with terrapins in brackish water, turtles in freshwater or the sea, and tortoises on land.
A group of turtles is sometimes called a 'bale' or a 'turn.'
Galápagos tortoises have distinct shell shapes, dome-shaped or saddleback, reflecting adaptations to different island habitats and food availability.
Saddleback Galápagos tortoises have a shell that flares upward at the front, letting them reach higher vegetation like cactus pads.
Charles Darwin's observations of differing Galápagos tortoise shell shapes across islands contributed to his early thinking on natural selection.
Some turtles are believed to detect polarized light and use it, alongside the Earth's magnetic field, to help orient during long migrations.
Turtle shell size varies enormously, from the palm-sized speckled padloper tortoise to the giant leatherback sea turtle.
The speckled padloper tortoise, native to South Africa, is the smallest tortoise species, with adults reaching only around 3 inches long.
Small basking turtles, like musk and painted turtles, can often be seen stacked on top of each other on logs during the day.
A turtle's carapace (top shell) and plastron (bottom shell) are connected by a bony structure on each side called a bridge.
Two Soviet tortoises aboard the Zond 5 spacecraft became the first Earth animals to circle the Moon and return safely, beating Apollo 8's crewed flight by months in 1968.
Turtles have no external ears; sound is instead picked up as vibrations through bones in the skull.
Sea turtles hear low-frequency sounds fairly well but have limited high-frequency hearing compared to many other animals.
A turtle's age can sometimes be roughly estimated by counting growth rings on its scutes, similar to tree rings, though the method becomes unreliable in older individuals.
Spiny softshell turtles have a long, pig-like snout adapted for breathing while mostly buried in sand or mud.
Musk and mud turtles live mostly aquatic lives and bask far less often than sliders or painted turtles.
Turtles symbolize longevity, wisdom, and stability in many cultures, appearing in mythology from ancient China to Indigenous traditions across North America.
Pet turtles can carry Salmonella bacteria, which is why handwashing after handling turtles or their tank water is recommended.
Since 1975, U.S. law has banned the sale of turtles under 4 inches long as pets, largely because of Salmonella risk to young children.
Snapping turtles can survive in fairly polluted or low-oxygen waters that many other turtle species cannot tolerate.
Tortoises that eat fruit help disperse seeds, since many seeds pass through their digestive tract unharmed and get deposited elsewhere.
Giant tortoises are considered 'ecosystem engineers' on islands like the Galápagos and Aldabra, shaping vegetation growth through their grazing habits.
A turtle's shell is built from both bone and fused vertebrae, meaning it literally carries its ribcage and spine as its outer armor.
Green sea turtles can weigh 300 to 400+ pounds and are the largest hard-shelled (non-leatherback) sea turtle species.
Hawksbill turtles are considered one of the most colorful sea turtles, with translucent amber, orange, and brown patterning on their shells.
The Kemp's ridley's main nesting site is a single beach in Rancho Nuevo, Mexico, making it especially vulnerable to localized threats.
